items.title: House price from square footage
items.scenario: A startup has 50,000 past home sales with features (sq ft, bedrooms, zip code encoded) and label y = sale price in dollars. Product needs a dollar estimate for new listings.
items.explanation: Classic linear regression: ŷ = Xw + b with continuous y. MSE on training prices is the default OLS objective; MAE/RMSE on held-out data evaluate deployment quality (see Loss Functions and RMSE & MAE).

items.title: Advertising spend vs revenue
items.scenario: Marketing teams track weekly spend on TV, radio, and online ads alongside revenue. Leadership asks: "If we add $10k to online ads, how much revenue lift do we expect?"
items.explanation: Each channel weight in w is a marginal effect (holding others fixed). Linear regression gives interpretable coefficients — though causality requires careful study design beyond this topic.

items.title: Student study hours vs exam score
items.scenario: An ed-tech app logs hours studied and practice problems completed per student; y is final exam score out of 100.
items.explanation: Two-feature linear model ŷ = w₁·hours + w₂·problems + b. MSE finds the best-fit plane through noisy (x, y) points; one outlier student with score 20 despite 100 hours pulls w under pure OLS.

items.title: Sensor calibration line
items.scenario: A factory calibrates a temperature sensor: known reference temperatures y_ref vs raw ADC readings x. They fit a line to map reading → true °C.
items.explanation: Simple univariate regression (d = 1). Normal equation with two or more calibration points yields w and b; often nearly zero training error if the sensor is linear in range.

items.title: Baseline model in a Kaggle regression tab
items.scenario: Before trying XGBoost or neural nets, a team runs sklearn LinearRegression on standardized features to establish an RMSE floor on the leaderboard.
items.explanation: Linear regression is the fast, interpretable baseline for continuous targets. If it is close to complex models, the relationship may be mostly linear; large gaps suggest nonlinearity or interactions.
